The forces are coplanar (in the same plane). (a) What is the resultant (net force) of these two force vectors? (b) Find the magnitude and direction of this net force.
Answer: a. $$\mathbf{\vec{F}}_{\text{net}} = 5.0 \mathbf{\hat{i}} + 10.0 \mathbf{\hat{j}} \text{N}$$ ; b. the magnitude is $$F_{\text{net}} = 11 \text{N}$$ , and the direction is $$\theta = 63 \text{°}$$

Question: Two teams of nine members each engage in a tug-of-war, pulling in opposite directions on a horizontal rope. Each of the first team’s members has an average mass of 68 kg and exerts an average force of 1350 N horizontally on the ground as they pull on the rope. Each of the second team’s members has an average mass of 73 kg and exerts an average force of 1365 N horizontally on the ground as they pull on the rope in the opposite direction. (a) What is the magnitude of the acceleration of the two teams, and which team wins? (b) What is the tension in the section of rope between the teams?
o3 Answer:
Let  

• Team 1 (the “left” team) : 9 people, 68 kg each, push on the ground with 1350 N each.  
• Team 2 (the “right” team) : 9 people, 73 kg each, push on the ground with 1365 N each.

The rope is taken as light so its mass can be ignored.

------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  Acceleration and the winning team
------------------------------------------------------------
External horizontal forces on the complete system (both teams + rope) are the friction forces exerted by the ground on the two teams.

Total force on Team 1 (to the left)          : F₁ = 9 (1350 N) = 1.215 ×10⁴ N (left)  
Total force on Team 2 (to the right)         : F₂ = 9 (1365 N) = 1.2285 ×10⁴ N (right)

Net external force on the whole system  
F_net = F₂ – F₁ = 1.2285×10⁴ N – 1.215×10⁴ N = 1.35×10² N to the right

Total mass of the two teams  
M = 9(68 kg) + 9(73 kg) = 612 kg + 657 kg = 1.269×10³ kg

Acceleration of the system  
a = F_net / M = 135 N / 1269 kg ≈ 0.11 m s⁻² to the right.

Because the acceleration is toward Team 2’s side, Team 2 wins.

------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Tension in the rope between the teams
------------------------------------------------------------
Take Team 1 alone as the system.

Forces on Team 1 (positive right):

T  –  F₁  = m₁ a

where  
m₁ = 612 kg, F₁ = 1.215 ×10⁴ N (leftward), a = 0.106 m s⁻² (rightward)

T = F₁ + m₁a  
  = 1.215×10⁴ N + (612 kg)(0.106 m s⁻²)  
  ≈ 1.215×10⁴ N + 66 N  
  ≈ 1.22×10⁴ N

(The same result is obtained using Team 2.)

Tension in the rope ≈ 1.2 ×10⁴ N.

------------------------------------------------------------
Answers
(a) Acceleration ≈ 0.11 m/s² toward Team 2; Team 2 wins.  
(b) Tension in the rope ≈ 1.2 ×10⁴ N.

(b) What force applied upward along and parallel to the ramp would allow the block to move with constant velocity?

 Perpendicular to the ramp: mg cos θ

mg cos θ = (2.0 kg)(9.8 m s⁻²) cos 30° = 19.6 N × 0.866 = 17 N

### Given Information:
*   Mass of the block ($$m$$) = 2.0 kg
*   Angle of the ramp ($$\theta$$) = 30°
*   The ramp is "perfectly smooth," which means there is no friction.
*   Acceleration due to gravity ($$g$$) = 9.8 m/s²

### Strategy
The first step in any force problem is to draw a free-body diagram to identify all the forces acting on the object. We will then resolve these forces into components that are parallel and perpendicular to the ramp's surface. Finally, we will apply Newton's Second Law ($$\sum F = ma$$) to each direction.

**1. Free-Body Diagram and Coordinate System**

It's most convenient to tilt our coordinate system so that the x-axis is parallel to the ramp (we'll set "down the ramp" as the positive direction) and the y-axis is perpendicular to the ramp.

The forces acting on the block are:
*   **Weight (W or Fg):** Acts straight down. Its magnitude is $$mg$$.
*   **Normal Force (N):** Acts perpendicular to the ramp's surface, pointing away from it.

We need to break the weight vector ($$W$$) into components:
*   **$$W_x$$:** The component of weight parallel to the ramp, pointing down the ramp.  $$W_x = mg \sin(\theta)$$.
*   **$$W_y$$:** The component of weight perpendicular to the ramp, pointing into the ramp. $$W_y = mg \cos(\theta)$$.



---

### (a) What is the block’s acceleration down the ramp and the force of the ramp on the block?

**1. Calculate Acceleration ($$a$$)**

We apply Newton's Second Law in the x-direction (parallel to the ramp).
$$\sum F_x = ma_x$$

The only force acting in the x-direction is the parallel component of the weight, $$W_x$$.
$$mg \sin(\theta) = ma_x$$

Notice that the mass ($$m$$) cancels out.
$$a_x = g \sin(\theta)$$
$$a_x = (9.8 \text{ m/s}^2) \sin(30^\circ)$$
$$a_x = (9.8 \text{ m/s}^2)(0.5)$$
$$a_x = 4.9 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The acceleration of the block down the ramp is **4.9 m/s²**.

**2. Calculate the Force of the Ramp on the Block (Normal Force, $$N$$)**

The "force of the ramp on the block" is the normal force. We apply Newton's Second Law in the y-direction (perpendicular to the ramp). Since the block is not accelerating off the ramp or into it, the acceleration in the y-direction ($$a_y$$) is zero.
$$\sum F_y = ma_y = 0$$

The forces in the y-direction are the normal force ($$N$$, in the positive direction) and the perpendicular component of weight ($$W_y$$, in the negative direction).
$$N - W_y = 0$$
$$N = W_y$$
$$N = mg \cos(\theta)$$
$$N = (2.0 \text{ kg})(9.8 \text{ m/s}^2) \cos(30^\circ)$$
$$N = (19.6 \text{ N})(0.866)$$
$$N \approx 17.0 \text{ N}$$

The force of the ramp on the block is **17 N** (rounded to two significant figures).

---

### (b) What force applied upward along and parallel to the ramp would allow the block to move with constant velocity?

The key phrase here is **"constant velocity."** According to Newton's First Law, an object moving at a constant velocity has an acceleration of zero ($$a=0$$). This means the net force on the object must be zero.

Let's call the new upward force $$F_{applied}$$. This force acts up the ramp, in the negative x-direction of our chosen coordinate system.

We apply Newton's Second Law in the x-direction again:
$$\sum F_x = ma_x = 0$$

The forces in the x-direction are now the parallel component of weight ($$W_x$$, down the ramp) and the applied force ($$F_{applied}$$, up the ramp).
$$W_x - F_{applied} = 0$$
$$F_{applied} = W_x$$
$$F_{applied} = mg \sin(\theta)$$
$$F_{applied} = (2.0 \text{ kg})(9.8 \text{ m/s}^2) \sin(30^\circ)$$
$$F_{applied} = (19.6 \text{ N})(0.5)$$
$$F_{applied} = 9.8 \text{ N}$$

An applied force of **9.8 N** upward along the ramp would balance the downward pull of gravity, resulting in zero net force and allowing the block to move at a constant velocity.
